original: A herd of 14 elephants returns home from a 500 km (300 mile) trek across China, leading authorities to evacuate 150,000 people out of their path
Jesse Owensis part of the American 4 x 100 m relay team that wins gold in a world record time of 39.8 seconds at the Berlin Olympics; his fourth gold medal of the Games
Jamaican sprint superstarUsain Boltwins the 200m at the London Olympics in 19.32 seconds, becoming the first to win the 100m and 200m double in consecutive Olympics
Smokey Bear debuts as the US Forest Service spokesperson for fire prevention; named after NYC firefighter Smokey Joe Martin
American swimmerMichael Phelpsswims 1:53.36 to win the men's 200 m butterfly gold medal at the Rio de Janeiro Olympics, beating Japan's Masato Sakai by 0.04 seconds for his 20th Olympic gold medal
Just one day after 8/8/88, the number 888 pops up as the winning lottery combination in New York
Construction of the Tower of Pisa begins, and it takes two centuries to complete
Edmonton Oilers tradeWayne Gretzkyto LA Kings for $15-$20 million
Landmark UN IPCC climate report is a "Code Red for humanity", rise of 1.5°C now certain; catastrophic change can still be avoided if the world acts quickly

American swimmerKatie Ledeckywins her second gold medal of the Rio de Janeiro Olympics in the women's 200 m freestyle with a time of 1:53.73, ahead of Sarah Sjöström of Sweden and AustralianEmma McKeon
Peter Docter, American animator, and film director (Monsters, Inc; Up; Soul), born in Bloomington, Minnesota
Adventure comedy film "Pee-wee's Big Adventure," starringPaul Reubens, is released
Satchel Paigeis inducted into the Baseball Hall of Fame
Robert Gray's Columbia Rediviva returns to Boston after a 3-year journey as the first American ship to circumnavigate the globe
Otis Tufts patents the first passenger elevator in the US
Jen Pawol (43) becomes first female to umpire an MLB baseball game when she joins the crew at Truist Park as Atlanta Braves host doubleheader against Miami Marlins
Hungary's Katinka Hosszú swims an Olympic record of 2:06.58 in the women's 200 m individual medley, winning her third gold medal of the Rio de Janeiro Olympics

US wins all three medals in women's fencing sabre event at the Beijing Olympics, the first US podium sweep of a fencing event since 1904; Mariel Zagunis wins gold ahead of Sada Jacobson and Rebecca Ward
Helen Stephens claims the women's sprint double at the Berlin Olympics, winning the 100 m and participating as part of the victorious American 4 x 100 m relay team
South Africa celebrates the first National Women's Day
Future six-weight boxing world champion Oscar De La Hoya of the US defeats German Marco Rudolph on points to win the lightweight gold medal at the Barcelona Olympics
Cuban boxer Félix Savón wins the first of three consecutive Olympic heavyweight gold medals when he dominates Nigerian David Izonritei on points at the Barcelona Games

MLB Phillies pitcher Michael Lorenzen throws a no-hitter in his first career complete game, a 7-0 win over the Washington Nationals, and outfielder Weston Wilson makes his MLB debut, hitting a home run in his first at-bat at Citizens Bank Park in Philadelphia
Russian spacecraft Soyuz TM-10 launches
USSR launches Luna 24, the last lunar flight to date from Earth
113°F (45°C) recorded in Perryville, Tennessee (state record)
Hungarian swimmer Ferenc Csik wins the 100 m freestyle gold medal in 57.6 seconds at the Berlin Olympics, beating Japanese pair Masanori Yusa and Shigeo Arai
NHL refuses the merger of six World Hockey Association clubs

MLB Philadelphia Phillies pitcherJim Bunningcontinues perfectly against the New York Mets until two outs in the 5th inning in the first match-up since throwing a perfect game in June when Joe Christopher beats out a bunt; Bunning masters the Mets for 15 innings over the two games
American transcendentalistHenry David Thoreaupublishes "Walden"
David A. Huffman, American computer scientist, creator of Huffman coding, born in Ohio (d. 1999)
Yves Coppens, French anthropologist (co-discovered 'Lucy' skeleton), born in Vannes, France (d. 2022)
Juanes [Juan Esteban Aristizábal], Colombian singer and songwriter, winner of 24 Latin Grammy Awards, born in Carolina del Príncipe, Antioquia, Colombia
Jean Piaget, Swiss pioneer developmental psychologist and zoologist, born in Neuchâtel, Switzerland (d. 1980)
Tetsuko Kuroyanagi, Japanese television personality and children's author, born in Nogisaka, Tokyo
Yinka Shonibare, British Nigerian artist (Nelson's Ship in a Bottle), born in London, England
Harry Mills, American baritone jazz and pop singer and vocal trumpet (The Mills Brothers - "Paper Doll", "Up A Lazy River", "The Glow-Worm"), born in Piqua, Ohio (d. 1982)
William T. G. Morton, American dentist who first used ether as a surgical anaesthetic (HOF 1920), born in Charlton, Massachusetts (d. 1868)
First US steam engine train runs from Albany to Schenectady, NY
USSR launches Mars 7
CaptainJames Cookreaches Cape Prince of Wales, Bering Strait
Jonathan Homer Lane, American astrophysicist who mathematically analyzed the Sun as a gaseous body, born in Geneseo, New York (d. 1880)
Max Neuhaus, American classical musician and experimental percussionist (sound sculptures), born in Beaumont, Texas (d. 2009)

WWII: Port Chicago Mutiny - mass work stoppage by munition loaders at Port Chicago Naval facility concerned with conditions after a recent explosion in Port Chicago, California; 258 men are convicted of various insubordination charges, including 50 who are tried for mutiny, found guilty, and sentenced to 15 years of hard labor; exonerated in 2024
Animated character Betty Boop debuts in Max Fleischer's cartoon "Dizzy Dishes"
First horses arrive in Hawaii
Italian rider Mario Ghella beats Reg Harris of Great Britain for the gold medal in the cycling sprint final at the London Olympics
STS 41-D vehicle moves out to the launch pad
LA Rams beat Dallas Cowboys 28-27 in London (NFL expo)
New Zealand reaches 100 days without community transmission of COVID-19
Rudolf Dieselof Germany obtains patent #608,845 for his internal combustion engine, later known as the diesel engine

A giant inflatable chicken resembling US PresidentDonald Trumpis placed outside the US White House as a political protest; Trump is out of town at the time [Note: photo shows return visit to National Mall in October of 2018]
Thomas Telford, Scottish civil engineer (designed and constructed Menai Bridge, Wales in 1819-26), born in Eskdale, Dumfriesshire, Scotland (d. 1834)
Brother André [Alfred Bessette], Canadian Roman Catholic lay brother and saint (St Joseph’s Oratory, Montreal), born in Mont-Saint-Grégoire, Quebec, Canada (d. 1937)
Daniel Keyes, American author (Flowers for Algernon), born in Boca Raton, Florida (d. 2014)
Jack DeJohnette, American Jazz drummer (Skyline), born in Chicago, Illinois
Kurtis Blow [Walker], American rapper (Krushgroove - "The Breaks"), born in New York City

Rod Brind'Amour, Canadian ice hockey center (Stanley Cup 2006 Carolina Hurricanes captain; NHL All Star 1992; Selke Trophy 2006, 07) and coach (Jack Adams Award 2021; Carolina Hurricanes), born in Ottawa, Ontario
A-Mei [Kulilay Amit], Taiwanese Puyuma singer known as the "Queen of Mando-Pop", born in Beinan, Taitung, Taiwan
Bulgaria is founded as a Khanate on the south bank of the Danube after the defeat of the Byzantine armies of Emperor Constantine IV near the Danube Delta
